title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Residents fight nonstop hum in court while local leaders weigh power and water costs.

lines.text: Good morning and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Thursday, July 16, and we're starting with a lawsuit that just landed in Cass County over nonstop noise from a new hyperscale facility.
That Dowagiac case feels like it could set a tone for how communities push back when the hum never stops.
Exactly, and it ties right into the bigger fights playing out across the state right now on power, water, and local control.
Let's dig into that Dowagiac story first.
Neighbors along Louise Avenue filed a class-action suit claiming the constant low-frequency hum from the new data center never lets up, day or night.
What are they actually asking for in the lawsuit?
Relief from what they call an unbearable intrusion on daily life, including possible mitigation measures or limits on operations.
This isn't the first time noise has come up in these fights, right?
No, it's a recurring complaint wherever these large facilities go in near homes, and it often leads to the same questions about enforcement.
How does this connect to the rezoning battle in Taylor?
Taylor residents are organizing against a rezoning request that could open the door to large-scale centers near neighborhoods, citing noise, traffic, and power demand as top worries.
So the pushback there is trying to stop it before it even starts.
Yes, and local officials may have to add stricter conditions or reconsider the proposal altogether.
Pere Marquette Township just advanced a moratorium too.
They did, halting new proposals while they draft permanent rules on roads, power lines, and property values.
How many other townships are watching this approach?
Several in Mason County and beyond are tracking it closely as a possible model for their own pauses.
Governor Whitmer's pledge plan is also circulating.
Her proposal asks companies to commit to covering their own power and water costs instead of passing them to ratepayers.
What's the reaction from local leaders so far?
Some call it a good first step, but others say it needs binding rules to really protect communities.
That gets to the core issue of who pays for the infrastructure.
Right, and it directly affects electric bills, water supplies, and how towns plan for growth.
What kind of scale are we talking about with these facilities?
A single large campus can draw enormous power and water, which is why farmland conversion and utility upgrades keep coming up in every debate.
And the new state bills introduced this week?
They would require data centers to meet standards on power use, water consumption, and workforce practices to avoid subsidizing growth through higher resident bills.
If those pass, how would it change where projects get sited?
It could reshape the map by making some locations less attractive without those protections in place.
Saline Township just approved a smaller tax break for the OpenAI campus.
Yes, after the company scaled back its ask, the forgone revenue dropped to about one-ninth of the original proposal.
Still means less for roads, schools, and emergency services though.
Exactly, and it sets the terms for how much the township contributes going forward.
Yesterday's stories show the momentum building too.
Warren's council voted to seek a one-year moratorium amid power and noise concerns.
Freedom Township residents successfully blocked a billion-dollar project that would have used huge amounts of power and water.
And the University of Michigan's proposed $1.25 billion center is drawing more local pushback as details emerge.
Whitmer also urged developers to sign the voluntary pledge yesterday.
Yes, framing it as a way to shield ratepayers and water supplies while multiple townships consider their own restrictions.
So the Dowagiac lawsuit lands right in the middle of all this.
It does, and it highlights how noise can become the flashpoint that forces broader conversations about enforcement and siting.
What happens next in the Dowagiac case?
The class-action moves forward in court, and it could influence how other communities respond when similar complaints arise.
Do you see more lawsuits like this if the hum complaints keep growing?
It's possible, especially in places where facilities sit close to homes and existing noise rules feel insufficient.
How does this affect property values in the short term?
Nearby residents often cite concerns that constant noise could depress values, which is why moratoriums and zoning fights keep referencing it.
And the farmland angle?
Large campuses often convert agricultural land, raising questions about long-term land use and local food systems.
Water use is another big one.
Cooling systems can require significant withdrawals, which ties directly into the governor's pledge and the new legislative standards.
Power demand is the elephant in the room for utilities.
Yes, and that's why the Michigan Public Service Commission meeting today on data center power contracts and rate impacts matters so much.
Listeners will want to know how that plays out.
We'll keep an eye on it and bring updates as they develop.
Back to the quick hits, the OpenAI tax break decision sets a precedent for scaled-back incentives.
It does, and it shows how communities can negotiate smaller revenue losses while still hosting projects.
The state bills on shielding residents from utility costs feel like the bigger policy play.
They would set statewide standards that could prevent the kind of ratepayer impacts seen in other states.
Taylor's rezoning fight could force the city to add conditions or walk it back.
And Pere Marquette's moratorium gives residents time to weigh in before permanent rules are set.
Whitmer's pledge is now in circulation among townships facing their own fights.
Some see it as helpful guidance, others want enforceable commitments instead.
On deck today, the Michigan Public Service Commission meets on data center power contracts and rate impacts.
That's at the usual time and place for anyone who wants to attend or watch the livestream.
It should give a clearer picture of how utilities plan to handle the growing demand.
Listeners can check the agenda for public comment opportunities.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Dowagiac Neighbors Sue Over Data Center Noise as More Townships Hit Pause
